text: A well-stocked pantry forms the foundation of food security. These staples store for 1-5+ years when kept cool, dry, and sealed: white rice (indefinite if sealed with oxygen absorbers), dried beans and lentils (5+ years), oats (2-3 years), pasta (2-3 years), salt (indefinite), sugar (indefinite), honey (indefinite — crystallization is normal, gently warm to re-liquefy), powdered milk (2-3 years), cooking oil (1-2 years), vinegar (indefinite), baking soda (indefinite), dried herbs and spices (1-3 years), canned goods (2-5 years past the date, as long as the can is not bulging, rusted through, or deeply dented).
section: Long-Term Food Storage > Shelf-Stable Pantry Basics

text: For truly long-term storage (10-25+ years), use Mylar bags with oxygen absorbers inside 5-gallon food-grade buckets. Fill Mylar bags with dry goods (moisture content must be below 10% — do not use this method for brown rice, nuts, or high-fat foods). Place 300cc oxygen absorbers per gallon of volume (2000cc for a 5-gallon bag). Seal the Mylar bag with a hot iron or hair straightener — the seal must be airtight. Place the sealed Mylar bag inside the bucket and seal the bucket lid. Label with contents and date. Store in a cool, dark location — every 10°F decrease in temperature roughly doubles shelf life.
section: Long-Term Food Storage > Mylar Bag Storage

text: Use the FIFO method: First In, First Out. When adding new supplies, place them behind existing stock. Mark purchase dates clearly on all items. Can rotation shelves (commercially available or DIY from angled shelving) automatically move the oldest cans to the front. Build your stored food supply from items your family actually eats — this ensures rotation happens naturally through regular use. Aim to rotate through your entire pantry within 1-2 years. Discard any canned goods that are bulging, leaking, or have off odors when opened.
section: Long-Term Food Storage > Rotation Systems

text: Whole grain berries (wheat, rye, spelt) store for 25+ years in sealed Mylar with oxygen absorbers — far longer than flour, which goes rancid within 1-2 years. A hand-cranked grain mill converts stored grain into fresh flour for bread, pasta, and baking. Manual grain mills range from simple hand-crank models ($50-100) to high-quality burr mills ($200-400) that can also crack corn and process other grains. Freshly milled flour has more nutrients and better flavor than commercial flour. Store at least 25-30 pounds of grain per person per month if relying on it as a primary calorie source.
section: Long-Term Food Storage > Grain Storage and Milling

text: Beyond food storage, store seeds for future growing seasons. Open-pollinated (non-hybrid) varieties can be saved and replanted year after year. Store seeds in airtight containers (mason jars with desiccant packets) in a cool, dark location — a refrigerator is ideal. Most vegetable seeds remain viable for 2-5 years: tomato (4-5 years), pepper (2-3 years), squash (4-5 years), beans (3-4 years), lettuce (1-3 years), corn (1-2 years). Test germination before depending on old seeds: place 10 seeds on a damp paper towel in a plastic bag, keep warm for 7-10 days, count how many sprout. Below 50% germination, plant extra or acquire fresh seed.
section: Long-Term Food Storage > Garden Seed Storage

text: Good fencing is the foundation of livestock management. For cattle, use high-tensile wire or barbed wire (4-5 strands, posts every 12-15 feet). For goats, woven wire or cattle panels at least 4 feet high — goats will find any gap. For chickens, use hardware cloth (1/2 inch mesh) buried 12 inches underground to prevent predator digging. Electric fencing is effective and economical for most livestock: a solar-powered charger with polytape or polywire provides a flexible, movable fence system ideal for rotational grazing. Test your electric fence regularly — vegetation growing on the wire grounds out the charge.
section: Livestock Management > Fencing Fundamentals

text: Divide your pasture into paddocks and rotate animals every 1-4 weeks depending on grass growth. After animals leave a paddock, the grass needs 21-45 days of rest to regrow (longer in dry conditions, shorter in the growing season). Benefits of rotation: prevents overgrazing, breaks parasite cycles, builds soil fertility, and increases pasture productivity by up to 70% compared to continuous grazing. Follow cattle with chickens — they scratch through manure, eat fly larvae, and spread nutrients evenly. Start with at least 4 paddocks to maintain a proper rotation cycle.
section: Livestock Management > Rotational Grazing

text: In cold climates, livestock need dry shelter more than warmth — a three-sided shed open to the south provides wind protection while allowing ventilation (moisture buildup causes more health problems than cold). Increase feed by 10-20% in winter — animals burn more calories maintaining body temperature. Ensure water doesn't freeze: heated water buckets, insulated troughs, or twice-daily ice breaking. Chickens reduce egg production in winter due to decreased daylight — supplemental light (a timer-controlled bulb providing 14-16 hours of total light) maintains production. Hay consumption for goats: approximately 4-5 pounds per day per adult goat in winter.
section: Livestock Management > Winter Livestock Care

text: Learn to recognize signs of illness: lethargy, loss of appetite, isolating from the herd, abnormal droppings, discharge from eyes or nose, limping, or rapid breathing. Maintain a basic livestock first aid kit: wound spray (Blu-Kote or equivalent), electrolyte powder, thermometer (normal temp varies: cattle 101.5°F, goats 102-103°F, chickens 105-107°F), syringes for administering medication, hoof trimmers, and clean bandaging material. Establish a relationship with a large-animal veterinarian before you need one in an emergency. Quarantine any new animals for 2-3 weeks before introducing them to your existing herd.
section: Livestock Management > Basic Animal Health

text: For a self-sustaining homestead, plan breeding cycles carefully. Chickens: a rooster per 8-12 hens produces fertile eggs; hens go broody (sit on eggs) naturally or use an incubator (21-day incubation). Goats: breed does in fall (September-November) for spring kidding; gestation is 150 days; a doe typically produces 1-3 kids. Rabbits: does can be bred at 6 months; gestation is 31 days; a doe can produce 6-8 litters per year with 6-8 kits per litter. Keep detailed breeding records including dates, parentage, and outcomes to improve your herd genetics over time.
section: Livestock Management > Breeding and Reproduction

text: Begin garden planning 6-8 weeks before the last expected frost date. Start warm-season crops indoors under lights: tomatoes, peppers, and eggplant need 6-8 weeks of indoor growing before transplanting. Direct-sow cold-hardy crops as soon as soil can be worked: peas, lettuce, spinach, radishes, and kale tolerate frost and can be planted 4-6 weeks before the last frost date. Harden off indoor seedlings by gradually exposing them to outdoor conditions over 7-10 days before transplanting. Work compost into garden beds (2-4 inches) before planting. Test soil and amend based on results — most vegetables prefer pH 6.0-7.0.
section: Seasonal Gardening Guide > Spring Planning and Planting

text: Water deeply and less frequently rather than shallow daily watering — this encourages deep root growth. Most vegetables need 1-1.5 inches of water per week. Water in the morning to reduce evaporation and disease. Mulch 2-4 inches deep around plants to retain moisture, suppress weeds, and regulate soil temperature. Side-dress heavy feeders (tomatoes, corn, squash) with compost or balanced fertilizer every 3-4 weeks. Scout for pests daily — hand-pick large insects, spray aphids with a strong water jet, use row covers to prevent moths from laying eggs on brassicas. Harvest regularly to encourage continued production — a single overripe zucchini signals the plant to stop producing.
section: Seasonal Gardening Guide > Summer Maintenance

text: Plant fall crops in mid to late summer: broccoli, cabbage, Brussels sprouts, carrots, beets, turnips, and lettuces. Many crops improve in flavor after light frost — kale, carrots, parsnips, and Brussels sprouts convert starch to sugar as a natural antifreeze response. Extend the season with row covers (floating fabric that adds 4-8°F of frost protection), cold frames (bottomless boxes with transparent lids), or low tunnels (hoops covered with plastic). Harvest winter squash after the first light frost but before a hard freeze — cure in a warm dry area for 10 days before storing. Save seeds from your best plants for next year (open-pollinated varieties only).
section: Seasonal Gardening Guide > Fall Harvest and Extension

text: After the final harvest, plant cover crops (winter rye, crimson clover, hairy vetch) to protect and improve soil over winter. Cover crops prevent erosion, suppress weeds, fix nitrogen (legumes), and add organic matter when turned under in spring. Alternatively, spread 4-6 inches of leaves or straw over beds as a winter mulch. Clean and sharpen tools before storage — oil metal surfaces to prevent rust. Drain and store hoses before freezing temperatures. Review the season's successes and failures — keep a garden journal noting varieties, planting dates, yields, and pest issues. Order seeds in winter while selection is best.
section: Seasonal Gardening Guide > Winter Preparation

text: Establish perennial food plants that produce year after year without replanting. Fruit trees (apple, pear, cherry, plum) take 3-5 years to produce but then yield for decades. Berry bushes (blueberry, raspberry, blackberry, elderberry) establish in 1-2 years. Asparagus produces for 15-20 years after a 2-year establishment period. Rhubarb, horseradish, Jerusalem artichoke, and many herbs (rosemary, thyme, oregano, chives) are perennial and require minimal maintenance. Plant perennials in dedicated beds where they will not be disturbed by annual garden tilling. Mulch heavily and prune as needed.
section: Seasonal Gardening Guide > Perennial Food Systems

text: A properly maintained well provides reliable water for decades. Have your well water tested annually for bacteria (total coliform and E. coli), nitrates, pH, and total dissolved solids. Spring testing is especially important as snowmelt and heavy rains can introduce contaminants. Inspect the wellhead annually: the casing should extend at least 12 inches above ground level, the cap should be securely sealed, and there should be no cracks or gaps where surface water could enter. Keep the area around the wellhead clear of chemicals, fertilizers, and animal waste — maintain a minimum 50-foot setback from potential contamination sources.
section: Water Infrastructure for Homesteads > Well Maintenance

text: If your well tests positive for bacteria, shock chlorinate it. Calculate the volume of water in your well (well depth in feet × gallons per foot based on casing diameter — a 6-inch casing holds 1.5 gallons per foot). Add enough unscented household bleach to achieve 200 ppm chlorine — approximately 3 pints of bleach per 100 gallons of water. Pour the bleach into the well, connect a hose from an outdoor faucet back into the well and recirculate for 30 minutes to mix thoroughly. Open each faucet in the house until you smell chlorine, then close them. Let the solution sit in the well and plumbing for 12-24 hours (do not use the water during this time). Flush the system by running water through an outdoor hose onto a non-vegetated area until the chlorine smell dissipates. Retest in 1-2 weeks.
section: Water Infrastructure for Homesteads > Shock Chlorination

text: A complete rainwater harvesting system includes: gutters and downspouts (clean regularly), a first-flush diverter (discards the first 1-2 gallons of dirty roof runoff per 1,000 sq ft of roof), a leaf screen or filter basket, storage tank (food-grade polyethylene, fiberglass, or concrete), overflow pipe, and a distribution system (gravity-fed or pump). Size your tank based on roof area and local rainfall. A 2,000 sq ft roof in an area with 1 inch of rain collects approximately 1,250 gallons. For drinking water use, filter (minimum 1 micron) and disinfect (UV sterilizer or chlorination) all collected rainwater. Check local regulations — some jurisdictions restrict rainwater harvesting.
section: Water Infrastructure for Homesteads > Rainwater Collection Systems

text: If your water source (spring, tank, pond) is uphill from your home, gravity provides free water pressure. You need 2.31 feet of elevation for every 1 PSI of pressure — a typical household needs 20-40 PSI, requiring the source to be 46-92 feet above the point of use. Use HDPE (high-density polyethylene) pipe rated for your pressure needs. For every 100 feet of horizontal pipe, add approximately 5% to your pressure calculations to account for friction loss. Use 1-inch or larger pipe for main supply lines to reduce friction. Install a shutoff valve at the source and a pressure-reducing valve at the house if elevation provides excessive pressure. Bury pipe below the frost line to prevent freezing.
section: Water Infrastructure for Homesteads > Gravity-Fed Distribution

text: A farm pond provides water for irrigation, livestock, fire suppression, and potentially household use (with treatment). Site ponds in natural drainage areas with clay soil to minimize seepage. A typical half-acre pond 8-10 feet deep holds approximately 1.3 million gallons. Compact the dam in 6-inch lifts with heavy equipment. Install a bottom drain and an overflow spillway sized for a 25-year storm event. For a cistern (underground water tank), reinforced concrete or food-grade plastic tanks buried below the frost line store water reliably. A 1,500-gallon cistern provides a 2-week supply for a family of four at conservative usage (50 gallons per person per day).
section: Water Infrastructure for Homesteads > Pond and Cistern Construction
